description: Reflected XSS: user-controlled `name` and `bio` query params are interpolated into the HTML response without escaping. An attacker can craft a URL with malicious script tags in either field to execute arbitrary JavaScript in a victim's browser. Severity: High.

dimensions.name: finds_bug
dimensions.description: Does the review correctly identify the seeded vulnerability? Score 1.0 if the LLM names the issue as XSS (cross-site scripting); 0.5 if it identifies the area but mislabels it; 0.0 if it misses the vulnerability entirely.

dimensions.name: severity_accuracy
dimensions.description: How accurately does the LLM rate the severity? Compare against the expected true_severity in the reference. Score 1.0 if exactly correct, 0.5 if off by one tier (e.g., said Medium when truly High), 0.0 if rated Low or not-a-concern.

dimensions.name: actionability
dimensions.description: Did the LLM give concrete, specific remediation steps that a developer could act on directly? Specific approaches (HTML escaping function, templating engine with auto-escape, CSP headers) score higher; vague guidance scores lower.

dimensions.name: no_false_alarms
dimensions.description: Did the LLM avoid inventing additional bugs that aren't actually present in the code? Score 1.0 if the review only flags real issues. Penalize spurious 'might also consider...' findings.

expected_remediation: Use a templating engine with auto-escape (EJS, Handlebars, Pug)
Manually HTML-escape user input before interpolation (he.encode() or escape-html package)
Add a Content-Security-Policy header to limit damage from any future XSS
Consider a framework like React or Vue that escapes by default
expected.clarity_anchor: A clear review names 'XSS' or 'reflected cross-site scripting', identifies both vulnerable fields (name AND bio), and demonstrates the attack with a concrete example like ?name=<script>alert(1)</script>.
